turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We have two opinions to announce this morning. Justice Jackson has the opinion of the Court in Case 22-721, McElrath versus Georgia.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Under Georgia law, a jury’s verdict in a criminal case can be set aside if it is repugnant, meaning that it involves "affirmative findings by the jury that are not legally and logically possible of existing simultaneously." The Supreme Court of Georgia found that this case implicated that rule. Petitioner Damien McElrath was charged with three crimes for the same homicide: malice murder, felony murder, and aggravated assault. A jury found that McElrath was not guilty by reason of insanity with respect to malice murder, but found him guilty but mentally ill on the two other counts. Georgia’s Supreme Court concluded that because these two verdicts were impossible of existing simultaneously, both needed to be nullified under the state’s repugnancy doctrine, and it thereby authorized McElrath’s retrial. In his Petition to this Court, McElrath maintained that the Fifth Amendment’s double jeopardy clause prevents the state from retrying him for the crime that had resulted in the not guilty by reason of insanity finding. And for the reasons laid out in the opinion we file with the Clerk today, we agree. The double jeopardy clause prohibits a defendant from being retried after a verdict of acquittal has been rendered. Our cases recognize that a verdict of not guilty by reason of insanity, like the one the jury rendered here, is an acquittal, because it reflects that the government has failed to come forward with sufficient proof of the defendant’s capacity to be responsible for the relevant crime. Georgia argues that under its repugnancy doctrine, no acquittal occurred because there was no valid verdict in this case. But whether an acquittal has occurred for purposes of the double jeopardy clause is a question of Federal Law, a state’s characterization of a verdict does not control that determination. And, as just discussed, we find that the jury’s verdict here satisfies the federal double jeopardy standard. Moreover, under Federal Law, the fact that the jury’s not guilty by reason of insanity verdict on the malice murder count was accompanied by verdicts on other counts that appeared to rest on inconsistent findings is of no moment. The double jeopardy clause prohibits second guessing an acquittal for any reason. As a result, our cases hold that an acquittal is an acquittal even when it is inconsistent with other verdicts that a jury renders. And Georgia is mistaken with respect to its argument that the bar to second-guessing an acquittal applies only to general verdicts, since there is no way to ascertain the true basis of the jury’s decision when evaluating a general verdict, while it is possible to do so with a special verdict. To the contrary, our cases prohibit any speculation about the reasons for a jury’s verdict once there has been an acquittal, even when there are specific findings that provide a factual basis for such speculation, because doing so would improperly delve into the juror’s deliberations. We simply cannot know why the jury in McElrath’s case acted as it did, and the Double Jeopardy Clause forbids us to guess. For these reasons, and others explained in the Court’s opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Supreme Court of Georgia. The Court’s opinion is unanimous. Justice Alito has filed a concurrence.
